coredata.dc:title: Microwave-assisted synthesis of luminescent and biocompatible lysine-based carbon quantum dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2016 The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Choi, Yujin
Thongsai, Nichaphat
Chae, Ari
Jo, Seongho
Kang, Eun Bi
Paoprasert, Peerasak
Park, Sung Young
In, Insik
coredata.dc:description: Water-soluble carbon quantum dots presenting strong blue photoluminescence with the quantum yield up to 23.3% are simply prepared by microwave treatment of lysine as a building block within 5min in household microwave oven. The formation of lysine-based carbon quantum dots is estimated to proceed through the microwave induced thermal polyamidation and the subsequent carbonization reaction. More importantly, the control experiments using the other amino acids having linear structures reveals that the branched molecular structure of lysine (AB2 type polyamidation monomer) is one of prerequisite points to obtain strongly luminescent carbon quantum dots with high mass yield. Lysine-based carbon quantum dots are highly nontoxic and biocompatible, enabling bioimaging of cellular media with high degree of pysiological safety.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Lysine
Microwave
Branching
Bioimaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–vis spectra of aqueous solutions of lysine-based CQDs (0.1mg/mL) with different microwave operation time and (b) PL spectra of lysine-based CQD prepared from 5min of microwave operation (0.1mg/mL) upon the different excitation wavelength (the insets are photo images of CQDs under room light and under dark with the 365nm UV irradiation). (For interpretation of the references to color in text, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) TEM image, (b) HRTEM images of lysine-based CQD prepared from 4min of microwave operation (the inset is fast Fourier transform image of the corresponding HRTEM image), and (c) AFM image of lysine-based CQD and its height profiles for the selected lines.
(a) FT-IR (KBr pellet), (b) 1H NMR, and (c) 13C NMR spectra of lysine and lysine-based CQD (microwave operation for 4min).
(a) XPS survey scan, and high resolution XPS spectra of (b) O1s, (c) C1s, and (d) N1s binding peaks of lysine-based CQD (microwave operation for 4min).

(a) FT-Raman spectrum of lysine-based CQD prepared from microwave operation for 4min and (b) fluorescence images of CQD solutions in different solvents (the concentration of CQD solutions is 0.1mg/mL in all cases).

(a) Cell viability tests of lysine-based CQDs in different concentrations for KB and MDCK cells, and (b) confocal microscopy images (405nm light excitation) of MDCK cells in the presence of lysine-based CQD (CQDs prepared from microwave operation for 4min were used in all cases).
Schematic drawing for the preparation of CQDs based on the type of polyamidation monomers, AB2 and AB type.

head.ce:title: Microwave-assisted synthesis of luminescent and biocompatible lysine-based carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering and Department of IT Convergence (Brain Korea PLUS 21), Korea National University of Transportation, Chungju-Si, Chungbuk 380-702, Republic of Korea. Fax: +82 43 841 5220.

Corresponding author at: Department of Polymer Science and Engineering and Department of IT Convergence Brain Korea PLUS 21, Korea National University of Transportation, Chungju-Si, Chungbuk 380-702, Republic of Korea. Fax: +82 43 841 5420.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Water-soluble carbon quantum dots presenting strong blue photoluminescence with the quantum yield up to 23.3% are simply prepared by microwave treatment of lysine as a building block within 5min in household microwave oven. The formation of lysine-based carbon quantum dots is estimated to proceed through the microwave induced thermal polyamidation and the subsequent carbonization reaction. More importantly, the control experiments using the other amino acids having linear structures reveals that the branched molecular structure of lysine (AB2 type polyamidation monomer) is one of prerequisite points to obtain strongly luminescent carbon quantum dots with high mass yield. Lysine-based carbon quantum dots are highly nontoxic and biocompatible, enabling bioimaging of cellular media with high degree of pysiological safety.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, intensive research interests have been attempted to develop luminescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as the next generation carbon nanomaterials in addition to 0D fullerene, 1D carbon nanotube, and 2D graphene[1]. The promising optoelectrical performance of CQDs enables the replacement of heavy metal-based inorganic quantum dots (QDs) with CQDs in many applications including light-emitting diodes [2], photocatalysis [3], sensing [4], solar energy harvesting [5,6], bioimaging [7,8], and drug-delivery systems [9] due to their excellent biocompatibility, low environmental toxicity, superior photochemical stability, and easy surface functionalization.

Either “top-down” or “bottom-up” approach has been exploited for the synthesis of CQDs until now. However, “bottom-up” approach utilizing the polycondensation or self-assembly of biocompatible small molecules or polymers prevails “top-down” approach employing the chemical or physical breakage of carbon-rich materials in the consideration of environmental and physiological compatibility of prepared CQDs [10]. In “bottom-up” approach, the initiation and progress of CQD formation necessitates the incorporation of harsh reaction conditions such as combustion [11], thermal treatment [12], chemical carbonization [13], and acid/alkali-assisted ultrasonic or oxidation reactions [14].

Very recently, it has been unveiled that the microwave assisted pyrolysis of certain small molecules in aqueous media by household microwave oven successfully facilitates the formation of water-soluble CQDs with high photoluminescence quantum yield (QY) [15]. As the carbon sources, carbohydrates such as glucose have been extensively exploited. Few nm-sized CQDs exhibiting deep ultraviolet (DUV) or blue emission with the QY up to 11% have been prepared by microwave-assisted hydrothermal reaction of glucose within 11min [16]. Microwave treatment of glucose as the carbon source and polyethylene glycol (PEG) 1500 as the co-reactant for 10min successfully produced 2–4nm CQDs showing chemiluminescence in strongly alkaline condition [17]. Other carbohydrates (glycerol, glycol, sucrose, etc.) have been also utilized for the preparation of photoluminescent CQDs with the QY up to 9.5% through the microwave treatment in the presence of inorganic ions within a few minutes [18]. Additionally, polymerizable monomers through polycondensation reactions have been incorporated for the preparation of CQDs through the microwave assisted polymerization and pyrolysis. Mostly, polyamidation monomer sets of Ax (A: −COOH, x=3 in the case of citric acid) and By (B: −NH2, y=2 in the case of urea) type monomers have been utilized for the preparation of CQDs with enhanced fluorescence QY of 14% by the microwave treatment within several minutes through the dual roles of urea as N-doping precursors and surface passivation agents [19].

While the “bottom-up” approach based on the above microwave assisted pyrolysis of small molecules is facile, less energy/time consuming, and easily scalable for the preparation of highly fluorescent CQDs, the incorporation of biocompatible and naturally abundant carbon sources is still urgent especially for the bioapplications of CQDs [20,21]. In this study, lysine has been chosen as an alternative biocompatible precursor of CQDs, because lysine is one of naturally abundant amino acids present in high-protein foods such as eggs, meat, soy, beans, etc. [22]. Structurally, lysine is one of AB2 type monomers which can be polymerized to polylysine through the polyamidation reaction [23]. Because microwave assisted pyrolysis of polymerizable monomers incorporates not only the carbonization and CQD formation process but also the polymerization of monomers, the choice of proper monomers and the control of polymerization during the microwave operation is very critical for the successful formation of highly soluble and strongly photoluminescent CQDs. While A3 and B2 monomer sets in previous reports can induce gelation at the early stage of polyamidation reaction [24], AB2 type monomers such as lysine do not produce gelation along the polycondensation because no intramolecular cyclization is enabled [25]. Additionally, the utilization of AB2 monomers does not require the optimization of feeding ratio because both A and B functional groups are staying in single molecule, which simplifies the structural characterization of prepared CQDs. For a comparison, A3 and B2 type monomer sets require precise control of the feeding ratio between A3 and B2 monomers to maximize the fluorescence QY of resulting CQDs.

From all these considerations, it is motivating to investigate the microwave assisted pyrolysis of AB2 type monomers such as lysine to produce water soluble, highly fluorescent, and biocompatible CQDs. From the cooperative examination of the microwave assisted pyrolysis of AB type monomers, it is clear that the incorporation of “branching point” in the structure of polymerizable monomers should be inevitable for the successful formation of highly fluorescent CQDs. Lysine-based CQDs showed strong blue emission upon the irradiation of UV (365nm) light with the fluorescence QY of 23.3% together with excellent in-vitro non-cytotoxicity up to 1mg/mL, which facilitates the application of lysine-based CQDs for the bioimaging of cellular media.
l-Lysine was purchased from Sigma–Aldrich Corporation. Molecular weight cut-off membrane (MWCO, 3500Da) was purchased from Spectrum Laboratories, Inc.

Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectra were obtained with Optizen Alpha UV–vis Smart Spectrometer of Mecasys (South Korea). Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained using a PerkinElmer L550B luminescence spectrometer. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were taken with a Nicolet iS10 FT-IR spectrometer of Thermo Fischer Scientific Inc. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were obtained using a Multimode-N3-AM Scanning Probe Microscope of Bruker. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were obtained from a Tecnai G2 F20 field emission transmission electron microscope of FEI Corporation. 1H NMR and 13C NMR spectra were obtained using a Bruker Advance 400MHz spectrometer with deuterium oxide (D2O) as the solvent. Raman analysis was done with a LabRAM high-resolution UV/vis/NIR dispersive Raman microscope (Horiba John Yvon). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were obtained by using tetra probe base system (Thermo Fisher Scientific Co.). The multimode microplate reader Filter MaxF3 (Molecular Devices, LLC) was used for MTT assay and quantitative cellular accumulation. Confocal images were obtained An LSM510 confocal laser scan microscope (CLSM) of Carl Zeiss (Germany) at ×10 magnification to take images of stained cells.

Luminescent and biocompatible lysine-based CQDs was prepared form lysine by one-step microwave pyrolysis method. At first, lysine (1g) was dissolved in 5mL of deionized water in 250mL flat-bottomed flask. After loosely covering the top of the flask with polyethylene wrapping film, the flask was treated inside of household microwave oven (750W) for 4–6min (Keep the whole microwave oven inside of fume hood during the synthesis of lysine-based CQDs not to inhale the organic volatiles from the microwave pyrolysis!). While the aqueous lysine solution is treated, its color is changed from color-less solution to brown and finally to dark brown solution, indicating the formation of CQDs. After complete cooling to room temperature, 100mL of deionized water was added into the flask and the mixture was sonicated in bath sonicator for 30min, resulting in the complete extraction of CQD into the aqueous media. Centrifuge (4000rpm, 30min) was attempted to remove any insoluble precipitates or agglomerates from the CQD solution. Finally, brown lysine-based CQDs powder was obtained after freeze-drying of the dialysed CQD solution using 3500Da MWCO membrane for at least 3 days.

The cell viability of CQDs was measured using an [3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide] MTT assay method [26]. 200μL of KB and MDCK cells, at a density of 1×105 cells/mL, were placed in each well of a 96-well plate. Afterwards, the cells were incubated for 24h at 37°C in a humidified 5% CO2 atmosphere. To determine the cellular viability, a stock solution of lysine-based CQDs was dissolved in RPMI medium at a concentration of 1mg/mL and the stock solution was diluted up to 0.001mg/mL The media was removed and the cells were treated with different concentrations of the solution of lysine-based CQDs. The cells were then incubated as previously for another 24h. The media containing lysine-based CQDs were then replaced with 180mL fresh medium and 20μL stock solution containing 15mg of MTT in 3mL PBS, and incubated for another 4h. Finally, the medium was removed and 200μL solution of MTT solubilizing agent was added to the cells and accurate shaking was performed for 15min. The absorbance was measured at 570nm wavelength using a microplate reader (Varioskan® Flash, Thermo Electron Corporation). The relative cell viability was measured by comparing with the 96-well control plate containing only cells. Consequently, a MTT-mediated cell viability assay with KB and MDCK cells demonstrated more than 97% of cell viability in the concentration of lysine-based CQDs between 0.001 and 1mg/mL. After cell viability measurement, confocal images were obtained to confirm stained cells (cells imaging/dead cells/live and dead cells) by using laser scan microscope (CLSM) at 10× magnification.

The microwave assisted pyrolysis of lysine was simply attempted by putting flasks containing aqueous lysine solution with the lysine concentration of 16.7wt.% and operating the microwave irradiation for 4–6min without any other chemical agents and treatments. Strong fluorescence can be observed even after the microwave operation for 2min but the optimum microwave operation time was evaluated as 4min due to the highest mass yield of CDQs and fluorescence QY in this case (Table S1, ESI). After cooling to room temperature, lysine-based CQDs were extracted from the crude product by ultrasonication with the addition of extra water and the subsequent filtration to remove dark black insoluble by-product. Then, any free lysine and its oligomers or polymers were thoroughly removed by the dialysis in deionized water. After freeze-drying, about 0.3g of purified lysine-based CQD was obtained from 1g of lysine. The formation of CQDs by microwave-assisted pyrolysis of lysine might resemble the formation of CQDs by hydrothermal treatments of citric acid and ethylenediamine. The initial condensation of lysine produces the polymer-like clusters and further carbonization forms the carbogenic CQDs [27]. Actually, prolonged microwave-treatment of lysine more than 10min produced insoluble dark-black precipitates due to the uncontrollable carbonization. The resulting lysine-based CQD was highly dispersible in aqueous media, leaving no precipitation or floating particle even after staying for several months. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectrum of lysine-based CQDs showed strong absorption peaks at 208nm and weak broad absorption at around 290nm (Fig. 1 a). Both absorption peaks are regarded to originate from π–π* transition of π skeletons composed of CC/CO groups and n–π* transition of carbonyl groups in lysine-based CQDs. Lysine-based CQDs did not show any optical absorption above 400nm, which is also confirmed by observing colorless and transparent CQD solutions.

Fluorescence properties of lysine-based CQDs were evaluated by monitoring fluorescence spectra upon the modulation of excitation wavelength. Aqueous solutions of lysine-based CQDs showed the characteristic excitation wavelength-dependent emission regardless of microwave operation time (Fig. 1b) [28]. The peak emission wavelength (λ em) was definitely increased from 406 (purple) to 497nm (green) with the excitation wavelength of 320 and 440nm, respectively. This tunable emission of lysine-based CQDs prepared by the microwave-assisted pyrolysis is regarded to originate from the different particle sizes of CQDs and the distribution of different emissive sites on the surface of CQDs [29]. The maximum fluorescence intensity was observed at 432nm (blue) with the excitation with UV light (360nm). The measured fluorescence QY (determined by a comparative method using quinine sulfate in 0.1M H2SO4 showing QY of 54%) [30] of lysine-based CQDs with the emission at 432nm was decreased from 23.3% to 5.8% with the increasing time of the microwave treatment from 4 to 6min. Then, the fluorescence lifetimes (τ) of lysine-based CQDs was compared each other. While small organic molecules show fluorescence lifetimes in the order of ps, large fluorophores such as lysine-based CQDs in this study present fluorescence lifetimes with the order of ns (Fig. S1, ESI). Fluorescence decay of lysine-based CQDs showed multi-exponential decay with the average τ of 1.6ns regardless of microwave pyrolysis time, showing that both the lateral dimension and the fluorescence emission profile of lysine-based CQDs are similar. The occurrence of multi-exponential tailing in the fluorescence decay of lysine-based CQDs might result from the significant electronic interaction between emission sites of each CQD.

Then, the morphological features of lysine-based CQDs were carefully examined. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of lysine-based CQDs obtained from 5min of microwave operation revealed the presence of spherical particles with the diameter ranging from 5 to 10nm (Fig. 2 a). High resolution TEM (HRTEM) images showed the presence of multiple lattice structures within single CQD. Longer microwave treatment produced slightly bigger CQDs (Fig. S2, ESI). Fourier-transform image of above HRTEM images showed diffuse ring pattern with d spacing of 0.36nm that is slightly bigger than d spacing of graphite (0.34nm) (Fig. 2b) [31]. Therefore, lysine-based CQDs is regarded to be composed of amorphous-like carbon lattice. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images of lysine-based CQDs (Fig. 2c) showed the presence of spherical particles with the thickness ranging 3–9nm, which is coincident with the previsou TEM analysis. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis of lysine-based CQDs only presented broad diffraction peaks centered at 2θ of 11.4° (0.77nm) and 20.4° (0.44nm), which confirms that lysine-based CQDs have carbon quantum dot- or carbon dot-like morphology rather than graphene quantum dot-like morphology (Fig. S3, ESI) [20].

Next, the structural features of lysine-based CQDs were examined. Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of lysine-based CQDs showed the characteristic peaks for OH/NH stretching (3343cm−1), CH stretching (2937cm−1), amide CO stretching (1653cm−1), NH bending (1553cm−1), and CH2 bending (1453cm−1) (Fig. 3 a) [32]. The build-up of amide linkages in FT-IR spectrum of CQDs clearly supports the progress of polyamidation reaction of lysine precursor through the microwave operation. Carboxylic acid groups of CQDs routinely appear higher frequencies than 1700cm−1 and above FT-IR analysis confirms the disappearance of carboxylic acid groups in lysine-based CQD [33]. Therefore, the surface of lysine-based CQDs is regarded to be passivated by rich amide bond linkages, which is critical for the preparation of highly fluorescent CQDs [34]. The end functional groups in the surface of lysine-based CQDs are regarded as primary amine or hydroxy groups (−NH2 or −OH). The presence of amine functionality in the surface of lysine-based CQDs is also confirmed by observing positive zeta-potential value of +21.4 and +38.7mV (Fig. S4, ESI). Because above FT-IR analysis is not conclusive for the confirmation of the build-up of CC double bond framework (tetra-substituted double bonds typically do not reveal CC stretching peaks in FT-IR spectra), both proton nuclear magnetic resonance (1H NMR) and carbon nuclear magnetic resonance (13C NMR) analysis of lysine-based CQDs were attempted. 1H NMR spectrum of CQDs revealed significant shift of proton peaks ranging from 4.10 to 1.24, which shows that most protons in CQDs are aliphatic protons rather than aromatic or vinyl protons (Fig. 3b). While aromatic proton peak was found at 7.93ppm, the peak integral was too much low. Also, no vinyl proton was observed between 6 and 5ppm. More conclusive information was obtained from 13C NMR analysis. While newly formed peaks of carbonyl carbons are clearly observed between 180 and 160ppm, no peak was found between 150 and 100ppm, which confirms the absence of both aromatic and vinyl carbons in lysine-based CQDs (Fig. 3c). Mainly, aliphatic carbon peaks ranging between 60 and 20ppm were observed with slightly shifted arrangement compared with pristine lysine. Therefore, it is estimated that lysine-based CQDs have no or very little amounts of CC framework.

The detailed compositional information of lysine-based CQDs was obtained from X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis. XPS survey scan of lysine-based CQDs showed the presence of C, N, and O atoms with the relative at.% of 77.0, 10.7, and 12.3, respectively (Fig. 4 a). High resolution XPS spectrum for O1s binding peak with binding energy of 530.5eV showed symmetrically shaped peak, which confirms that the environment of O atoms is uniform in CQDs (Fig. 4b). In the case of C1s binding peak at 284.2eV, deconvolution of raw C1s binding peak presented CC/CC (85.2%), CO or CN (5.6%), and COO or CON (9.2%) bonding (Fig. 4c). In N1s binding peak, it is estimated that NH bonding and CON bonding were overlapped (Fig. 4d). From all these characterization, the build-up of amide linkages upon the microwave treatment is clearly observed. The inclusion of nitrogen atoms into CQDs is regarded to be critical for accomplishing enhanced fluorescence QY of CQDs possibly through the surface passivation. Actually, the surface passivation of carboxylic acid functionalized CQDs by diamine-containing oligo- or polyethylene glycol (PEG) such as 4,7,10-trioxa-1,13-tridecanediamine (TTDDA) or PEG1500N has been attempted to dramatically improve the weak luminescence properties and water solubility of certain carbon dots simultaneously [33,34]. In the case of lysine-based CQDs, CQDs are self-passivated due to the presence of rich amine or amide functional groups in the surface CQDs. Also, inclusion of N-doping into carbon core framework might contribute to the enhancement of fluorescence QY [35].

Raman analysis is very informative for the interpretation of morphological features of carbon-based nanomaterials such as carbon nanotube and graphene [36]. Interestingly, Raman spectrum of the thick films of lysine-based CQDs on Si wafer revealed no Raman scattering peaks for G band (primary in-plane vibrational mode) and D band (different in-plane vibration) at 1580 and 1350cm−1, respectively, upon the irradiation of 514 and 785nm (Fig. S5, ESI). Because the strong fluorescence of CQDs could interfere the Raman scattering, near IR laser (1064nm) was used to prevent electronic absorption and thus fluorescence of lysine-based CQDs [37]. Instead of G and D band peaks, the characteristic Raman scattering peaks of amide III, alkyl/vinyl/aromatic, and amide I were clearly observed at 1594, 1446, 1280cm−1, respectively (Fig. 5 a) [38]. This finding is also corresponding to the previous NMR analysis. Therefore, CQDs prepared from the microwave pyrolysis of lysine are regarded to possess rich amide groups in carbonized framework. Rich hydroxy groups stay mainly at the surface of lysine-based CQDs presenting high dispersibility of CQDs in various protic solvents including water, methanol, and ethanol, and poor solubility in nonpolar solvents such as chloroform and toluene (Fig. 5b). Lysine-based CQD showed complex solvatochromism in different solvents. In alcoholic solvents, CQDs showed positive solvatochromism probably due to the less specific stabilization of the polar excited states in less polar solvents compared with water (Fig. S6, ESI). However, solvatochromism in much nonpolar solvents such as toluene is difficult to estimate due to the poor solubility of polar lysine-based CQDs in nonpolar solvents.

The high aqueous solubility and QY of lysine-based CQDs together with the physiological friendliness of pristine lysine prompts us to investigate the bioapplications lysine-based CQDs toward bioimaging. Typically, it is reported that the carbonized framework of CQDs are not toxic [39]. Lysine-based CQDs showed very good biocompatibility as cell imaging agents. A MTT-mediated cell viability assay with KB and MDCK cells demonstrated more than 97% of cell viability in the concentration of lysine-based CQDs between 0.001 and 1mg/mL, revealing that lysine-based CQD is a strong candidate for biological applications as non-toxic materials (Fig. 6 a). Because the cytotoxicity of CQDs is regarded to originate from the surface passivation agents on the surface of CQDs, the low cytotoxicity of lysine-based CQD might result from the robust self-passivated surface structure of CQDs [40]. After the incubation of MDCK cells with lysine-based CQDs for 24h, most MDCK cells were completely labeled with blue-emitting CQDs, showing that the successful uptake of lysine-based CQDs through endocytosis (Fig. 6b). These results promote lysine-based CQDs as strong candidates to be exploited in cellular imaging for diagnosis purposes.

Finally, the role of lysine as one of AB2 type polyamidation monomers for the high yield synthesis of CQDs was evaluated by attempting comparative synthesis of CQDs from AB type analogues, 6-aminohexanoic acid and glycine, whose molecular structures are preserved in lysine in part (Fig. 7 ). Interestingly, CQD synthesis was totally infeasible with the use of above AB type amino acids instead of lysine through the microwave assisted pyrolysis. The successful synthesis of CQDs with high QY and mass yield from lysine presents the usefulness of lysine as amino acid-based building blocks for the construction of CQD-related nanomaterials. Compared with other AB-type amino acids, lysine is an AB2 type amino acid and possesses branching point in its molecular structure. Therefore, the initial polyamidation process induced by heating generated by hot spots during microwave treatment could provide highly branched polylysine which is much preferable for the construction of CQDs with 3-dimensional geometry in the accompanying dehydration/carbonization process. While CQD synthesis from AB type amino acids such as 3-(3,4-dihydroxyphenyl)-l-alanine, l-histidine, and l-arginine has been reported, high thermal treatment up to 300°C was adopted for the production of CQDs [41]. In comparison, the microwave assisted synthesis of lysine-based CQDs tested in this study is very simple, cost-saving, and mass-scalable. Additionally, various other amino acids can be easily incorporated along with lysine to modulate the surface and electronic environments of prepared CQDs.

Water-dispersible, strongly fluorescent, and physiologically compatible CQDs are simply prepared from microwave pyrolysis of lysine with high mass yield more than 30%. The maximum QY of lysine-based CQDs reaches up to 23.3% in addition to high cell viability more than 97% for KB and MDCK cells. As the proper choice of starting materials for the bottom-up synthesis of CQDs through microwave pyrolysis, our study reveals that the incorporation of branching point(s) in the molecular structure of starting materials is one of key factors for the production of CQDs with high QY. Through the use of lysine as the key material for the synthesis of amino acid-based CQDs, various combination of lysine and other amino acids might provide simple, fast, cost-saving, and mass-scalable synthesis of CQDs with tunable surface and electronic environments through the microwave assisted pyrolysis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea(NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education (2015R1D1A3A01020192) and a Grant (No. 10049064) by Industrial Technology Innovation Program funded by Ministry of Trade, Industry & Energy(MI, Korea).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jiec.2016.12.002.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
